techniques.evidence_quote: this attack could occur from different attack paths – including application vulnerabilities or zero-day exploits such as Log4Shell – not only from misconfigurations
the attacker tried (unsuccessfully) to create a new user by executing the CreateUser command.
the attacker tried to hide some of his activities by deleting the SES identity by executing the DeleteIdentity command.
An attacker was able to exfiltrate the Lambda's environment variables and export them into their attacking machine (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_SESSION_TOKEN).
The attack started with the GetCallerIdentity command. This command is equivalent to whoami, as it provides information about the entity the credentials are associated with.
The IAM enumeration included two API calls, which were denied due to lack of permissions: ListAttachedRolePolicies, ListRolePolicies

the attacker started an enumeration on different services in different regions... Some of the main services and API calls that were executed were: Storage Enumeration... EC2 Enumeration... Network Enumeration... Logging Enumeration... Backup Enumeration... SES Enumeration...

the attacker was able to successfully enumerate SES. Therefore, the attacker launched a phishing attack by abusing the cloud email service, which included executing commands such as VerifyEmailIdentity and UpdateAccountSendingEnabled.

attack_chain.description: Attacker achieved code execution within the Lambda function context through an application vulnerability (exact exploit unspecified; Log4Shell-style or similar RCE implied).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: this attack could occur from different attack paths – including application vulnerabilities or zero-day exploits such as Log4Shell – not only from misconfigurations

attack_chain.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
attack_chain.description: Attacker exfiltrated the Lambda function's temporary IAM credentials from its environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_SESSION_TOKEN) and exported them to the attacker-controlled machine.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: An attacker was able to exfiltrate the Lambda's environment variables and export them into their attacking machine (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_SESSION_TOKEN).

attack_chain.description: Using the stolen credentials from an external IP (50.82.94.112), the attacker called GetCallerIdentity to identify the stolen identity, account ID, and credential type — equivalent to 'whoami'.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attack started with the GetCallerIdentity command. This command is equivalent to whoami, as it provides information about the entity the credentials are associated with.

attack_chain.description: Attacker attempted IAM enumeration via ListAttachedRolePolicies and ListRolePolicies to understand privilege scope; both calls were denied.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The IAM enumeration included two API calls, which were denied due to lack of permissions: ListAttachedRolePolicies, ListRolePolicies

attack_chain.description: Attacker performed broad enumeration across S3, EC2, VPC networking, CloudWatch/CloudTrail logging, EBS backup, and SES services across multiple regions to map the target account's architecture and find accessible resources.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker started an enumeration on different services in different regions... Some of the main services and API calls that were executed were: Storage Enumeration... EC2 Enumeration... Network Enumeration... Logging Enumeration... Backup Enumeration... SES Enumeration...

attack_chain.description: Attacker attempted to create a new IAM user (CreateUser) to establish a persistent backdoor; the call failed due to insufficient permissions.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker tried (unsuccessfully) to create a new user by executing the CreateUser command.

attack_chain.description: Attacker successfully enumerated SES (GetAccount, ListIdentities) and then abused it to register a sending identity and enable outbound email for a phishing campaign (VerifyEmailIdentity, UpdateAccountSendingEnabled).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker was able to successfully enumerate SES. Therefore, the attacker launched a phishing attack by abusing the cloud email service, which included executing commands such as VerifyEmailIdentity and UpdateAccountSendingEnabled.

attack_chain.description: Attacker deleted the SES sending identity (DeleteIdentity) to cover tracks and reduce evidence of the phishing infrastructure within the victim's account.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker tried to hide some of his activities by deleting the SES identity by executing the DeleteIdentity command.

initial_access_vector: Code execution achieved within an AWS Lambda function context, enabling exfiltration of the function's IAM temporary credentials from environment variables (exact exploit vector not specified; application vulnerability or misconfiguration implied)
persistence_mechanism: Attempted to create a new IAM user (CreateUser) for persistent backdoor access; call failed due to lack of permissions
privilege_escalation: No successful privilege escalation observed; IAM enumeration (ListAttachedRolePolicies, ListRolePolicies) and CreateUser were all denied due to insufficient permissions on the Lambda's role

nodes.description: Attacker achieved code execution within the Lambda function context through an application vulnerability (exact exploit unspecified; Log4Shell-style or similar RCE implied).
